metadata.title: Exploring a Language-Based Interest Assessment: Predicting Vocational Interests on Social Media Using Natural Language Processing

abstract: For more than a century, self-report inventories have been the traditional method for assessing vocational interests. Little research has examined the use of machine learning techniques, such as natural language processing (NLP), in interest assessment. This paper explores the extent to which natural language on social media can be used to predict individuals' self-ratings on eight basic interests representing the SETPOINT model: Agriculture, Engineering, Human Resources, Life Science, Management/Administration, Mechanics/Electronics, Media, and Social Science. Leveraging closed-(Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count; LIWC) and open-vocabulary NLP approaches (Latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic modeling), we analyzed 3.2 million Facebook posts from 2,834 participants who completed a 32-item basic interest measure. We found that the convergent validities of these NLP approaches for predicting vocational interest scores (LIWC: r = 0.19; LDA topic modeling: r = 0.24) are comparable to prior research on language-based personality assessments. Our study also revealed largely face-valid language markers that characterize different basic interests. Implications for developing language-based interest assessments for applied settings (e.g., career guidance and employee selection) and future research directions are discussed.

paragraphs: Vocational interests-defined as trait-like preferences for work activities and environments (Rounds & Su, 2014)-are among the most widely assessed constructs in applied psychology (Nauta, 2010;Nye, 2022;Su, 2020). Interests have long been used in career guidance to help people make academic and career-related decisions (e.g., Holland, 1997;Parsons, 1909;Strong, 1943), and recent research supports their validity for predicting educational and occupational choices (Hanna & Rounds, 2020). In the past decade, vocational interests have also gained traction as a part of hiring assessments (Nye & Hoff, 2023;Wille & De Fruyt, 2023) and job placement (Kirkendall et al., 2020). This is because interests can predict training and job performance (Nye et al., 2017;Van Iddekinge et al., 2011) and typically have smaller adverse impact than cognitive ability tests (Jones et al., 2022;Wee et al., 2023). Given the power of interests for predicting various career-related outcomes, effective and efficient interest assessments are particularly important to scholars and practitioners.

Over the last 100 years, interests have traditionally been assessed using self-report inventories (Nye, 2022;Parsons, 1909). However, there have been concerns about the use of selfreport interest measures, such as: a) lack of ecological validity due to items involving imagined and hypothetical activities (Roemer et al., 2021); (b) modest convergent validity among different measures, which can lead to inconsistent career recommendations (Chu et al., 2022); (c) faking, particularly in high-stakes settings (Wegmeyer & Speer, 2022); and d) lengthy and tedious assessment format, which can lead to inattentive responding (Tracey, 2020). These concerns highlight a need to explore alternatives to self-reports, such as using machine learning techniques (e.g., natural language processing) for interest assessment (Nye, 2022;Tracey, 2020).

sections.paper_id: Du et al. - 2025 - Exploring a Language-Based Interest Assessment Predicting Vocational Interests on Social Media Usin
sections.header_norm: exploring a language based interest assessment
paragraphs: Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), and big data (Oswald et al., 2020;Woo et al., 2020Woo et al., , 2024) ) have led to formal calls to examine the use of natural language processing (NLP) in interest assessment (Hanna et al., 2024;Nye, 2022). NLP refers to a range of computational techniques for analyzing natural language to achieve human-like linguistic processing for various tasks (Liddy, 2001). Research suggests that NLP methods for predicting psychological traits (e.g., Park et al., 2015;Tay et al., 2020) offer a promising approach to replacing time-consuming self-reports-they are unobtrusive, cheap, and relatively accurate. These NLP-based (i.e., language-based) assessments can also lead to efficiency gains and potentially automate the use of vocational interests in career guidance and workforce development (e.g., Putka et al., 2023).

To date, language-based assessments have focused mostly on personality prediction (e.g., Kern et al., 2014Kern et al., , 2019;;Park et al., 2015;Schwartz et al., 2013). For instance, studies have shown that Facebook or X (formerly Twitter) data are reasonably predictive of Big Five personality traits (r = .28-.39; Azucar et al., 2018), dark personality traits (r = .05-.29; Garcia & Sikström, 2014), and character strengths (r = .13-.51; Pang et al., 2020). Meta-analytic research found that social media language-based assessments displayed considerable convergence for predicting self-reported Big Five personality scores (mean r = 0.27 across the Big Five; Tay et al., 2020). In this regard, Park et al. (2015) has been considered as the "gold standard" for evaluating the convergence between self-reported and language-based personality assessments: they found convergent validities of r = .38-.46 (mean r = .41) for the Big Five personality traits (Tay et al., 2020, p. 833).

In terms of interest research, a recent study showed that NLP can be leveraged to accurately and efficiently estimate the interest profiles of jobs, as well as job characteristics (e.g., knowledge, skill, and abilities; Putka et al., 2023). Little research, however, has examined the use of NLP for assessing individuals' vocational interests. To understand if language-based interest assessments can be practically implemented for career guidance and organizational purposes, examining the accuracy (i.e., convergent validity) of NLP models for interest prediction is essential.

sections.paper_id: Du et al. - 2025 - Exploring a Language-Based Interest Assessment Predicting Vocational Interests on Social Media Usin
paragraphs: In this study, we investigate the validity of NLP approaches for predicting eight basic interests represented in the SETPOINT model (see Table 1; Su et al., 2019). SETPOINT is a hierarchical vocational interest model that comprises eight general (Health Science, Creative Expression, Technology, People, Organization, Influence, Nature, and Things) and 41 basic (i.e., lower-order) interests. While general interests represent broad occupational themes, basic interests are specific and homogeneous classes of work activities (i.e., narrower interest dimensions) that allow for more precise assessment of interest in careers (Bai & Liao, 2019;Su et al., 2019). Researchers Note. SETPOINT = Health Science, Creative Expression, Technology, People, Organization, Influence, Nature, and Things (Su et al., 2019). CABIN = Comprehensive Assessment of Basic Interests. CABIN has been shown to be a highly reliable basic interest measure (α = .90-.97 across the 41 subscales) and its structural validity was validated in a large, diverse sample of working adults (Su et al., 2019). The eight basic interests assessed in the current study were selected based on the highest factor loading on each of the SETPOINT dimensions. For a list of the 41 basic interest factors that load onto the SETPOINT dimensions, see Su et al. (2019).

have suggested that finer-grained basic interests can better capture labor market trends (e.g., emerging occupations) and provide a better representation of contemporary jobs (Hou et al., 2024;Su et al., 2019). For our study, we selected the eight basic interests-Agriculture, Engineering, Human Resources, Life Science, Management/Administration, Mechanics/Electronics, Media, and Social Science-because these interests exhibit the highest factor loadings on each SET-POINT dimension (Su et al., 2019). We used social media text data (i.e., Facebook posts) to develop our NLP models because social media text data are easily accessible and contains rich ecological footprints, which can be quantified to predict psychological traits (Schwartz et al., 2013). We combined both closed-and open-vocabulary NLP approaches to analyze three million Facebook posts from participants. A closed-vocabulary (top-down) approach uses an established dictionary or a set of word categories, which is developed a priori based on theory. The most widely used dictionary for language analysis in psychology is the Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) program (Pennebaker et al., 2015). We used the LIWC 2015 dictionary to develop a predictive model to assess vocational interests. In addition, we employed an open-vocabulary (bottom-up) approach-Latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic modeling-for interest prediction. An open-vocabulary approach is not limited to pre-determined word lists; it uses data-driven techniques that are "particularly suited for capturing the nuances of everyday psychological processes" (Eichstaedt et al., 2021, p. 418). Table 2 summarizes the NLP approaches and ML methods applied in the current study.

Our study has two major aims. First, we examine the convergent validity (i.e., predictive accuracy) of the NLP approaches by measuring the correlation between language-derived and self-reported interest scores. Second, using computational linguistic analysis, we explore the distinctive language correlates of vocational interests. Critically, identifying distinctive linguistic correlates of psychological traits (e.g., vocational interests) can allow future studies to predict that trait based on language alone without self-report surveys (Eichstaedt et al., 2021). This analysis also allows us to evaluate the face validity of the NLP approaches. To the best of our knowledge, little to no empirical research has examined the convergence and face validity of NLP approaches to interest assessment. The present study addressed this gap by developing and evaluating language-based models for predicting vocational interests. Because the current study is exploratory, we sought to answer the following research questions.

Research Question 1. What are the predictive accuracies of NLP approaches for assessing vocational interests (i.e., convergence/correlations between predicted and self-reported interest scores)? Research Question 2. What linguistic features (e.g., word categories and topics) are significantly and positively associated with each vocational interest?

sections.paper_id: Du et al. - 2025 - Exploring a Language-Based Interest Assessment Predicting Vocational Interests on Social Media Usin
Du et al. - 2025 - Exploring a Language-Based Interest Assessment Predicting Vocational Interests on Social Media Usin
paragraphs: The current study involves secondary data analysis of data collected from a larger study conducted by some of the authors of this article. In the original study, United States-based participants were recruited via the Qualtrics Panel. Participants received a financial incentive for answering a series of surveys and were invited to share access to their Facebook status updates. We obtained informed consent to access their Facebook posts and extracted all posts via the Facebook Graph API (Application Programming Interface). Three attention check questions were inserted in the surveys. Out of the 3,043 participants recruited, 2,955 participants (ages ranged 18-70 years: M = 43.47, SD = 12.30; 69.27% female; 64.33% with Bachelor's or higher degrees) passed all three attention checks; those who did not were excluded from the analysis. A total of 2,834 participants, who completed the vocational interest and demographic measures (e.g., age, gender) and had active accounts with more than 500 words on Facebook, were included in the language analysis. The original study received approval from the Institutional Review Board (IRB) of Purdue University. Secondary data analysis in the present study was exempted by the IRB of the University of Pennsylvania.

Participants received a financial incentive for answering a series of surveys and were invited to share access to their Facebook status updates. We obtained informed consent to access their Facebook posts and extracted all posts via the Facebook Graph API (Application Programming Interface). Three attention check questions were inserted in the surveys. Out of the 3,043 participants recruited, 2,955 participants (ages ranged 18-70 years: M = 43.47, SD = 12.30; 69.27% female; 64.33% with Bachelor's or higher degrees) passed all three attention checks; those who did not were excluded from the analysis. A total of 2,834 participants, who completed the vocational interest and demographic measures (e.g., age, gender) and had active accounts with more than 500 words on Facebook, were included in the language analysis. The original study received approval from the Institutional Review Board (IRB) of Purdue University. Secondary data analysis in the present study was exempted by the IRB of the University of Pennsylvania.

paragraphs: Vocational interests were measured by a 32-item adapted version of the Comprehensive Assessment of Basic Interests (CABIN; Su et al., 2019)-with four items measuring each of eight basic interests: Agriculture, Engineering, Human Resources, Life Science, Management/Administration, Mechanics/Electronics, Media, and Social Science. Participants indicated how much they like engaging in various work activities associated with these interests on a five-point Likert scale (1 = dislike a great deal; 5 = like a great deal; sample items are in Table 1). Cronbach's alphas of the eight basic interest scales range from .82 to .87 (means, SDs, and bivariate correlations of these interests are in Table S1, Supplemental Materials).

sections.paper_id: Du et al. - 2025 - Exploring a Language-Based Interest Assessment Predicting Vocational Interests on Social Media Usin
paragraphs: Using the happierfuntokenizer from the DLATK (Differential Language Analysis toolkit; Schwarz et al., 2017) software package, we tokenized all Facebook posts, splitting them into words, punctuation, and emojis. Using DLATK, we then extracted fine-grained and distinct language features from the participants' entire Facebook language. Feature Extraction LIWC Word Categories. We begin with a closed-vocabulary (top-down) NLP approach, LIWC (Pennebaker et al., 2015). LIWC contains 73 word categories that reflect various linguistic variables (e.g., pronouns) and psychological processes, including social relations (e.g., family and friends), personal concerns (e.g., money and religion), affective processes (e.g., positive and negative emotions), perceptual processes (e.g., see and hear), cognitive processes (e.g., insight and causation), and biological constructs (e.g., health and wellness).

LIWC is a word-count-based text analysis method. First, we computed the word frequencies for each participant and summed up the within-participant word frequencies for each of these LIWC categories. Next, we controlled for total word count by normalizing the within-participant word frequencies (i.e., dividing these values by the total number of words each participant has), thus obtaining the relative frequency of each LIWC category for each participant.

LDA Topics. We applied LDA topic modeling (Blei, 2012), which is an open-vocabulary (i.e., bottom-up, data-driven) approach. Topics are clusters of semantically related words that provide highly interpretable units of analysis (Eichstaedt et al., 2021). LDA is a generative probabilistic model that assumes that the posts contain a mixture of a fixed number of latent topics (decided in advance before analysis). We fixed this number to 2000 LDA topics, which were derived from a large corpus of approximately 19 million Facebook posts from the MyPersonality data set (Schwartz et al., 2013). We used the Mallet implementation (McCallum, 2002) to generate these topics automatically.

We then calculated the relative topic distribution of each participant aggregated across all posts. As an example, one topic identified in our study includes clusters of words that express gratitude (blessed, grateful, wonderful, fortunate, lucky, thankful, family). This means that a person who mentions the words blessed, grateful, and wonderful in their post would have a higher probability of "using" the gratitude topic described above.

Correlation Analysis. Using an exploratory technique known as Differential Language Analysis (DLA; Schwartz et al., 2013), we correlated vocational interests with the language features extracted from participants' Facebook posts: (a) LIWC word categories and (b) LDA topics. The aim of DLA is to identify the distinctive language correlates for each of the eight basic interests. We controlled for age and gender in all correlational analyses. Using word clouds, we visualized the LDA topics that were significantly and positively correlated with each vocational interest. All p-values in the correlational analyses were Benjamini-Hochberg corrected (Benjamini & Hochberg, 1995) to account for multiple comparisons.

Model Accuracy. We evaluated the accuracies of the NLP models for predicting vocational interests based on social media text data. We used each separate set of language features-LIWC word categories and LDA topics-as independent variables and self-reported interest scores as continuous dependent variables in (penalized) linear regression, controlling for age and gender. Given the high dimensionality of our language features, we used ridge regression. Ridge regression reduces model complexity and overfitting by applying a penalty that forces regression coefficients toward zero, while still allowing many predictors to avoid underfitting.

To calculate model accuracies, we used 5-fold cross-validation (CV). In 5-fold CV, the data are randomly split into 5 subgroups (folds), and a model is fit over four of the folds (training set, i.e., data used for training and hyperparameter selection). The trained model is then applied to the remaining fold (i.e., holdout or test data set), and its predicted outcome values (i.e., languageinferred interest scores) are compared with the actual user-reported values. Predictive accuracy is calculated as the Pearson correlation between the predicted and actual outcome values. This procedure is repeated round-robin until every fold serves as the test set once. The final predictive accuracy is the average of the five test set accuracies.

Supplementary Analysis: RoBERTa. Given recent advances in NLP and deep learning (Kjell et al., 2023), we conducted supplementary analysis using a state-of-the-art NLP approach, RoBERTa (Robustly Optimized Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers Pretraining Approach; Liu et al., 2019). RoBERTa is a pre-trained contextualized word embedding model. Unlike co-occurrence or count-based methods, RoBERTa contextualized word embeddings represent text as numeric vectors that can capture the distinctive meaning of words based on their contexts (e.g., can understand the difference between "travel from New York to Chicago" and "travel from Chicago to New York"; Nayak, 2019). The purpose of this supplementary analysis is to examine if RoBERTa can improve the predictive accuracies of LIWC and LDA topics for interest prediction.

To compute RoBERTa word embeddings, we averaged representations of each word in the post, with each word represented by its 11 th layer in the RoBERTa model. We used the huggingface RoBERTa-base pretrained model (Wolf et al., 2019) to compute the embeddings. We then input the RoBERTa embeddings as independent variables and self-reported interest scores as dependent variables in ridge regression, controlling for age and gender. We used the same 5-fold CV procedures to compute the prediction accuracy of the RoBERTa model.

paragraphs: In accordance with the SETPOINT model (Su et al., 2019), we specified an eight-factor CFA model to assess the construct validity of the 32-item basic interest measure adapted from CABIN. The CFA model showed acceptable model fit [χ 2 (436) = 3373.65, p < .001, RMSEA = 0.048. CFI = 0.94, TLI = 0.94; SRMR = 0.04; Hu & Bentler, 1999]. All items had relatively high loadings on the latent variables (standardized λs = 0.61-0.87). The CFA results show that the interest measure demonstrates good construct validity in the current study.

sections.paper_id: Du et al. - 2025 - Exploring a Language-Based Interest Assessment Predicting Vocational Interests on Social Media Usin
paragraphs: RQ1: What are the Predictive Accuracies of NLP Approaches for Assessing Interests?. Table 3 shows the model accuracies of the NLP approaches for assessing the eight basic interests (i.e., predictive accuracies of the LIWC and LDA models, which were trained using ridge regression and evaluated via 5-fold CV). The predictive accuracies of the LIWC word categories range from r = .12 for Agriculture to r = .25 for Media (mean predictive accuracy: r = .19). The predictive accuracies of the LDA topics range from r = .18 for Life Science to r = .31 for Mechanics/ Electronics (r = 0.24). For the supplementary analysis, the predictive accuracies of RoBERTa word embeddings range from r = .21 for Life Science to r = .36 for Mechanics/Electronics (r = 0.26). These results suggest that NLP models have modest-to-moderate convergent validity for predicting self-reported vocational interest scores.

RQ2: What Linguistic Features (Word Categories and Topics) are Significantly and Positively Associated With Each Interest?. Figure 1 shows the correlational analysis (i.e., DLA) results, which identified the linguistic features (i.e., LIWC word categories and LDA topics) that are significantly and positively correlated with each basic interest.

LIWC. LIWC is a closed-vocabulary (theory-driven) approach that can efficiently summarize concepts and effectively capture the style and content of language use (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010). Based on the DLA results for LIWC, distinct communication styles or content in language use were found for different interests. Interests that involve interpersonal facilitation (e.g., Human Resources and Management/Administration; Su et al., 2019) displayed a warm communication style (example word categories: affiliation, social processes, second-person pronouns). Interests that involve working with things and hands-on practical activities (e.g., Agriculture, Life Science, Engineering, Mechanics/Electronics) exhibited a direct and informal communication style (e.g., informal, anger, swear words). Interests related to the understanding of human behavior (Social Science) and creative expression (Media; c.f. Table 1) were related to content that signal complex linguistic and cognitive processes (e.g., hear, causation, insight, perceptual processes).

LDA Topic Modeling. LDA topic modeling is an open-vocabulary (empirically-driven) approach that is suitable for capturing the fine-grained nuances of people's everyday psychological processes (Eichstaedt et al., 2021). Agriculture was correlated to LDA topics that suggest an interest in farming (e.g., reap, plant, harvest), gardening (e.g., seeds, leaf, tree), and animals (e.g., birds, mouse, nest, bees, owl, flying, squirrel). Engineering showed significant associations with LDA topics related to space (e.g., nasa, outer, space, planet, launch, rocket, aliens) and ocean exploration (e.g., ancient, floating, ship, whale, discovery; see Figure 1).

Human Resources showed significant correlations with topics that signal the expression of positive emotions, such as gratitude (e.g., wonderful, blessed, thankful), affiliation (e.g., awwww, adore, admire, friendship) and happiness (e.g., joy, warmth, prosperity, peace, prosperity). Life Science was associated with LDA topics related to chemistry (e.g., acid, liquid, oxygen, element, chemical), animal husbandry (breed, shelter, zoo, animal, lions, elephant), and pets (doggy, adopt, vet, kittens, foster, stray). Management was related to LDA topics involving positive emotions (e.g., thankful, god's, blessing [in] disguise, gratitude), relaxation (e.g., enjoyin, chillin, chill, wifey), and mindfulness (e.g., live, life, fullest, cherish, embrace, moment). Mechanics/Electronics was associated with informal language (e.g., dork, nerd, kidding, lol, lmao), progressive verbs (e.g., tryin, makin, doin, startin, waitin, askin), and activities related to mechanical/car work (e.g., tires, engine, brakes, pump, replaced, wheel, rear, fuel, leak).

Media was associated with LDA topics related to movies (e.g., actors, director, plot, film, script, superb, Hollywood), fashion (e.g., models, fashion, magazine, designer, make-up), and creative writing (e.g., poetry, journal, articles, literature). Social Science was associated with topics related to spirituality (e.g., divine, mankind, compassion, human, beings, unity), philosophy (e.g., argument, intellectual, beliefs, logic, morals), politics (e.g., democracy, government, citizens, rights, liberty, politicians), and the social sciences (e.g., psychology, sociology, economics). In summary, the DLA results for LIWC and LDA topic modeling provide insights into the language behaviors associated with each vocational interest, which allow us to evaluate the facevalidity of these NLP approaches.

Figure 1 shows the correlational analysis (i.e., DLA) results, which identified the linguistic features (i.e., LIWC word categories and LDA topics) that are significantly and positively correlated with each basic interest.

LIWC. LIWC is a closed-vocabulary (theory-driven) approach that can efficiently summarize concepts and effectively capture the style and content of language use (Tausczik & Pennebaker, 2010). Based on the DLA results for LIWC, distinct communication styles or content in language use were found for different interests. Interests that involve interpersonal facilitation (e.g., Human Resources and Management/Administration; Su et al., 2019) displayed a warm communication style (example word categories: affiliation, social processes, second-person pronouns). Interests that involve working with things and hands-on practical activities (e.g., Agriculture, Life Science, Engineering, Mechanics/Electronics) exhibited a direct and informal communication style (e.g., informal, anger, swear words). Interests related to the understanding of human behavior (Social Science) and creative expression (Media; c.f. Table 1) were related to content that signal complex linguistic and cognitive processes (e.g., hear, causation, insight, perceptual processes).

LDA topic modeling is an open-vocabulary (empirically-driven) approach that is suitable for capturing the fine-grained nuances of people's everyday psychological processes (Eichstaedt et al., 2021). Agriculture was correlated to LDA topics that suggest an interest in farming (e.g., reap, plant, harvest), gardening (e.g., seeds, leaf, tree), and animals (e.g., birds, mouse, nest, bees, owl, flying, squirrel). Engineering showed significant associations with LDA topics related to space (e.g., nasa, outer, space, planet, launch, rocket, aliens) and ocean exploration (e.g., ancient, floating, ship, whale, discovery; see Figure 1).

Human Resources showed significant correlations with topics that signal the expression of positive emotions, such as gratitude (e.g., wonderful, blessed, thankful), affiliation (e.g., awwww, adore, admire, friendship) and happiness (e.g., joy, warmth, prosperity, peace, prosperity). Life Science was associated with LDA topics related to chemistry (e.g., acid, liquid, oxygen, element, chemical), animal husbandry (breed, shelter, zoo, animal, lions, elephant), and pets (doggy, adopt, vet, kittens, foster, stray). Management was related to LDA topics involving positive emotions (e.g., thankful, god's, blessing [in] disguise, gratitude), relaxation (e.g., enjoyin, chillin, chill, wifey), and mindfulness (e.g., live, life, fullest, cherish, embrace, moment). Mechanics/Electronics was associated with informal language (e.g., dork, nerd, kidding, lol, lmao), progressive verbs (e.g., tryin, makin, doin, startin, waitin, askin), and activities related to mechanical/car work (e.g., tires, engine, brakes, pump, replaced, wheel, rear, fuel, leak).

Media was associated with LDA topics related to movies (e.g., actors, director, plot, film, script, superb, Hollywood), fashion (e.g., models, fashion, magazine, designer, make-up), and creative writing (e.g., poetry, journal, articles, literature). Social Science was associated with topics related to spirituality (e.g., divine, mankind, compassion, human, beings, unity), philosophy (e.g., argument, intellectual, beliefs, logic, morals), politics (e.g., democracy, government, citizens, rights, liberty, politicians), and the social sciences (e.g., psychology, sociology, economics). sections.paper_id: Du et al. - 2025 - Exploring a Language-Based Interest Assessment Predicting Vocational Interests on Social Media Usin
paragraphs: The current study investigated the extent to which NLP can be used to predict eight types of basic interests on social media. Previously, this line of research focused on the automatic prediction of personality traits (Tay et al., 2020). We advance the literature by demonstrating that languagebased assessments can be extended to the field of vocational interests. Using closed-and open- vocabulary approaches, the NLP models for interest prediction-LIWC and LDA topic modeling-obtained mean predictive accuracies of r = .19 and .24, respectively. Supplementary analysis using RoBERTa, a state-of-the-art NLP approach, did not improve prediction substantially (r = .26)-suggesting that LIWC and LDA topic modeling are sufficiently robust NLP models for estimating users' interests.

Although results suggest that the convergent validities of these NLP models are relatively modest, the results should be interpreted in light of previous works on automatic personality prediction. In language-based assessments of Big Five personality traits on social media (e.g., Park et al., 2015;Schwartz et al., 2013), it is rare to attain r > .40 for the prediction models; their predictive accuracies usually fall below .30 -.40. For instance, even when state-of-the-art transformer-based contextual embeddings (i.e., BERT; Devlin et al., 2018) were used in Lynn et al. (2020), the predictive accuracy averaged across the Big Five personality traits was r = .39 (Eichstaedt et al., 2021). Furthermore, two meta-analyses found convergent validities of r = .27 between languagebased personality assessments and Big Five personality self-ratings (i.e., average r across the Big Five; Moreno et al., 2021; the same result was found for open-vocabulary approaches by Tay et al., 2020). Another meta-analysis revealed modest correlations between LIWC and Big Five personality traits (r = .23; |r| = .09-.32; Koutsoumpis et al., 2022). Therefore, the predictive accuracies of the NLP models achieved by the present study (r = .19-.26) are comparable to prior research on language-based personality assessment. We believe this is an encouraging result for future work on language-based interest assessments. Interestingly, there was a wide range of predictive accuracies across the eight basic interests (LIWC: r = .13-.25; LDA topics: r = .18-.31; RoBERTa: r = .21-.36), suggesting that some interests were better predicted than others. One possible explanation is that some interests were more manifest on social media (e.g., Mechanics/Electronics), while others were less observable on social media (e.g., Life Science, c.f. Table 3). This pattern aligns with past findings (Hickman et al., 2024) that language use tends to hold higher predictive validity for more observable (e.g., extraversion) than less observable personality traits (e.g., openness, neuroticism; Connelly & Ones, 2010). For instance, Mechanics/Electronics involves practical, hands-on activities that might be relatively more easily observed on social media, whereas Life Science tends to involve abstract, intellectual activities that might be less easily captured by social media language. This pattern is also consistent with McIntyre (2021), who found that tangible interests that involve working with things and people (i.e., Realistic 1 and Social) were perceived as more observable by human raters than abstract interests that involve working with ideas and data (e.g., Investigative and Conventional).

Further, the present study advances our understanding of the language markers that characterize vocational interests. Specifically, the DLA results provide novel and intuitive insights into the everyday language (i.e., topics and word categories) that individuals use depending on their interests. For instance, word clouds of the LDA topics suggest that a person who scores high in Media may enjoy fashion, watching films, and listening to music. Another person who scores high in Agriculture may enjoy activities related to nature, farming, and gardening (see Figure 1). Overall, our work reveals largely face-valid and distinct language profiles for different types of basic interests.

We note, however, that some basic interests are significantly related to the LIWC swear words category. Although this appears to suggest that the closed-dictionary LIWC categories are relatively less face-valid markers of vocational interests than the data-driven LDA topics, it is also possible that these relationships have substantive meaning. Specifically, a closer examination of the basic interests that are related to swear words include Agriculture, Engineering, Life science, and Mechanics/Electronics. This implies that swear words are generally more predictive of thingsoriented than people-oriented interests, which typically involve a high level of interpersonal facilitation (e.g., Management and Human Resources). Given meta-analytic evidence (Koutsoumpis et al., 2022) that swearing is negatively related to Agreeableness-a personality trait that promotes interpersonal facilitation (Mount et al., 1998)-it may not be surprising that swear words are more strongly associated with things-than people-oriented interests. Moreover, swear words serve a range of social functions that can be negative (e.g., aggression, frustration) or positive (e.g., humor, storytelling, in-group slang), depending on the contexts in which they are used (Holgate et al., 2018;Jay, 2009). Because LIWC is a count-based method that does not consider the contextual meaning of words, it is difficult to unpack the reasons why certain interests are related to the LIWC swear words category. Nevertheless, the results illustrated largely facevalid and distinctive language profiles for different types of basic interests.

sections.paper_id: Du et al. - 2025 - Exploring a Language-Based Interest Assessment Predicting Vocational Interests on Social Media Usin
paragraphs: Our study is a first step toward developing a language-based interest assessment. To the extent that language-based assessments can predict vocational interests as efficiently and effectively as personality traits (Moreno et al., 2021;Tay et al., 2020), there is potential to apply similar NLP approaches used in personality assessment for interest assessment. For instance, one study on 120,000 X (formerly Twitter) users representing 3,500 occupations found that language-derived Big Five personality scores can predict users' occupations fairly accurately (Kern et al., 2019). This implies that NLP-based assessments have potential to match people to occupations based on their personality and interests. In this regard, researchers have already developed an AI chatbot that could predict self-ratings of Big Five personality traits with relatively good convergent validity (r = .48; Fan et al., 2023). Thus, one possible application is the development of an AI chatbot that can assess interests automatically and provide users with tailored career recommendations, thus enhancing the process of person-job matching for job seekers. From a practitioner's (e.g., counseling or vocational psychologist's) perspective, this also means the potential to identify clients' interests more effectively and efficiently, thus enabling more personalized and timely feedback for career guidance and development purposes.

Moreover, NLP methods may be useful in facilitating large-scale psychometric assessment in applied settings (e.g., job placement) in an efficient and scalable manner. For instance, it is now possible for scientists to use social media text data to quantitatively represent thousands of individuals, give a subset of these individuals a vocational interest survey, and then use these survey responses to build NLP models capable of predicting the interests of other individuals using only their social media data (Abdurahman et al., 2024;Vu et al., 2020). Given that large organizations (e.g., the U.S. military) are increasingly using interest assessment to assign new employees to occupational roles (Kirkendall et al., 2020), there is similar potential to automatically generate interest scores based on applicants' text data (e.g., CVs and application essays), thereby reducing the need for costly and time-consuming data collection during job placement.

Recent evidence also suggests that NLP-based assessments can yield substantial cost savings for organizations that use psychological tests to make hiring decisions. Hickman et al. (2023) found that NLP methods can automate the scoring of assessment center exercises with sufficient validity to replace one or more human assessors-potentially reducing assessor costs of about US$160,000 a year for the employing organization. Regarding the use of vocational interests for employee selection (Nye & Hoff, 2023), one can imagine that complementing proprietary interest surveys with NLP-based interest assessments can help organizations enjoy similar cost savings. In addition, because incorporating interest assessments can help reduce adverse impact in hiring (Jones et al., 2022;Wee et al., 2023), NLP-based interest assessments may also be useful in increasing workforce diversity for organizations (Campion & Campion, 2023).

In sum, language-based interest assessments can be a cost-effective, scalable method to augment person-job matching for career seekers and employers, as well as enhance diversity hiring for organizations. Nevertheless, because the present study achieved only modest predictive accuracies for its NLP models, more validity evidence is needed before such novel methods can be practically implemented. We believe our study showcases the lower bound of what is achievable with these innovative NLP approaches, which hold good promise in automating vocational interest assessment for applied purposes.

Our study is a first step toward developing a language-based interest assessment. To the extent that language-based assessments can predict vocational interests as efficiently and effectively as personality traits (Moreno et al., 2021;Tay et al., 2020), there is potential to apply similar NLP approaches used in personality assessment for interest assessment. For instance, one study on 120,000 X (formerly Twitter) users representing 3,500 occupations found that language-derived Big Five personality scores can predict users' occupations fairly accurately (Kern et al., 2019). This implies that NLP-based assessments have potential to match people to occupations based on their personality and interests. In this regard, researchers have already developed an AI chatbot that could predict self-ratings of Big Five personality traits with relatively good convergent validity (r = .48; Fan et al., 2023). Thus, one possible application is the development of an AI chatbot that can assess interests automatically and provide users with tailored career recommendations, thus enhancing the process of person-job matching for job seekers. From a practitioner's (e.g., counseling or vocational psychologist's) perspective, this also means the potential to identify clients' interests more effectively and efficiently, thus enabling more personalized and timely feedback for career guidance and development purposes.

Moreover, NLP methods may be useful in facilitating large-scale psychometric assessment in applied settings (e.g., job placement) in an efficient and scalable manner. For instance, it is now possible for scientists to use social media text data to quantitatively represent thousands of individuals, give a subset of these individuals a vocational interest survey, and then use these survey responses to build NLP models capable of predicting the interests of other individuals using only their social media data (Abdurahman et al., 2024;Vu et al., 2020). Given that large organizations (e.g., the U.S. military) are increasingly using interest assessment to assign new employees to occupational roles (Kirkendall et al., 2020), there is similar potential to automatically generate interest scores based on applicants' text data (e.g., CVs and application essays), thereby reducing the need for costly and time-consuming data collection during job placement.

Recent evidence also suggests that NLP-based assessments can yield substantial cost savings for organizations that use psychological tests to make hiring decisions. Hickman et al. (2023) found that NLP methods can automate the scoring of assessment center exercises with sufficient validity to replace one or more human assessors-potentially reducing assessor costs of about US$160,000 a year for the employing organization. Regarding the use of vocational interests for employee selection (Nye & Hoff, 2023), one can imagine that complementing proprietary interest surveys with NLP-based interest assessments can help organizations enjoy similar cost savings. In addition, because incorporating interest assessments can help reduce adverse impact in hiring (Jones et al., 2022;Wee et al., 2023), NLP-based interest assessments may also be useful in increasing workforce diversity for organizations (Campion & Campion, 2023).

In sum, language-based interest assessments can be a cost-effective, scalable method to augment person-job matching for career seekers and employers, as well as enhance diversity hiring for organizations. Nevertheless, because the present study achieved only modest predictive accuracies for its NLP models, more validity evidence is needed before such novel methods can be practically implemented. We believe our study showcases the lower bound of what is achievable with these innovative NLP approaches, which hold good promise in automating vocational interest assessment for applied purposes.

sections.paper_id: Du et al. - 2025 - Exploring a Language-Based Interest Assessment Predicting Vocational Interests on Social Media Usin
paragraphs: The present study has several limitations that should be addressed by future research. First, this study used social media text data (i.e., Facebook posts) to develop its NLP models, but research suggests that predictive models trained on social media may not necessarily generalize well to applied settings (e.g., career guidance, personnel selection; Hickman et al., 2019). This is because social media text data are highly unstructured and not contextualized to these settings, which may have contributed to the modest convergence between language-derived and self-reported interest scores in the current study. Furthermore, there are legal, ethical, and privacy concerns about the use of social media data (Kosinski et al., 2015), as people might be opposed to organizations accessing their social media profiles without informed consent. Future research should explore more structured types of text data for interest assessment that typically do not give rise to such concerns (e.g., CVs and admission essays).

Second, people may still engage in impression management or faking in NLP-based psychological assessments (Campion et al., 2024), which may affect the accuracy of the languagederived interest scores. Although there is currently no empirical research on whether these assessments are resistant to faking, there is an emerging consensus that NLP-based assessments may be less susceptible to response distortion than traditional survey-based assessments (Campion et al., 2024;Fan et al., 2023). Because computer algorithms use a large quantity of input features derived from natural language and people may not know the content of these algorithms, it is highly improbable for them to deliberately manipulate these features to obtain 'higher' interest scores. Still, we recommend researchers to use suitable methods to control for impression management. For instance, in the current study, we controlled for self-presentation tendency via normalization (i.e., controlled for total word count by dividing within-participant word frequencies by their total number of words). Nevertheless, additional scholarship is needed to investigate the issue of faking in NLP-based psychological assessments.

Third, there may be potential for algorithmic bias in language-based interest assessments. This is an important limitation to consider if these assessments are used for practical purposes (e.g., career guidance and employee selection). For example, biases could occur if the same linguistic model produces different predicted scores for different sub-populations (e.g., gender, race, social media users vs. non-users), even though these groups have the same "ground-truth" level of interest scores (Tay et al., 2022). In addition, research has shown that the language markers of psychological constructs may differ across subgroups (e.g., age, gender, and race; Schwartz et al., 2013;Rai et al., 2024). Therefore, future research may investigate the potential for algorithmic biases in language-based interest assessments and possible ways to mitigate them.

Finally, the present study used a shortened version of CABIN (Su et al., 2019) to assess eight basic interests. As a result, its NLP models could predict only a subset of the 41 basic interests that represent SETPOINT and could not predict general interest dimensions that are more widely applied, such as RIASEC (Holland, 1997). Future studies may use more comprehensive measures (e.g., Strong Interest Inventory; Harmon et al., 1994) to evaluate the convergent validity of language-based assessments for different types of general and basic interests.

sections.paper_id: Du et al. - 2025 - Exploring a Language-Based Interest Assessment Predicting Vocational Interests on Social Media Usin
paragraphs: Effective and efficient assessment of interests are essential to researchers, counselors, and organizations. In the present study, we combined closed-and open-vocabulary methods to examine the validity of NLP approaches to predicting people's vocational interests on social media. We show that (a) vocational interests can be estimated from social media text data to a similar extent of accuracy as personality traits; and (b) language markers that characterize different basic interests are distinctive and largely face-valid. Our study provides preliminary evidence that NLP can be used to automatically assess vocational interests, but more validity evidence is needed before such assessments can be practically implemented. Given rapid advancements in AI and computational science, automated language-based interest assessments may become viable and scalable for practical use in the near future. We hope this study inspires new and exciting work that leverages NLP and big data approaches in vocational psychology and career assessment.
